Daily Crane Inspection Checklist: What OSHA Actually Requires

A detailed breakdown of every item your operators must inspect before each shift under 29 CFR 1926.1412(d) — and how to document it properly.

Every crane operator in the United States is required to perform a visual inspection before each shift. It is not optional. It is not a suggestion. It is a federal requirement under 29 CFR 1926.1412, and failing to comply can result in citations, fines, and — worst of all — preventable injuries or fatalities on your jobsite.

Yet many operators treat the daily crane inspection checklist as a formality. They rush through it, skip items, or rely on memory instead of a documented process. This article covers exactly what OSHA requires, item by item, so there is no ambiguity about what your daily crane inspection checklist needs to include.

What 29 CFR 1926.1412(d) Requires for Each-Shift Inspections

OSHA's crane inspection standards are split into several categories. The one that governs daily inspections is 29 CFR 1926.1412(d), titled "Each-shift inspection." This section applies to all cranes and derricks used in construction and mandates a visual inspection by a competent person before each shift the equipment is used.

The regulation distinguishes between two types of shift inspections:

  • Items that must be checked by the operator at the beginning of each shift. These are visual and functional checks the operator performs before starting work.
  • Items that require inspection before each shift by a competent person. In many cases the operator is the competent person, but the employer must ensure they meet that standard.

If a deficiency is identified, the equipment must not be used until the issue is corrected. There is no exception for deadlines, project pressure, or cost. For a broader overview of all inspection types and frequencies, see our guide on OSHA crane inspection requirements.

The Complete Daily Crane Inspection Checklist

The following checklist consolidates the items specified under 29 CFR 1926.1412(d) along with manufacturer-recommended checks that OSHA expects employers to follow. Every item below should appear on your daily crane inspection checklist.

1. Control Mechanisms

Before moving the crane, verify that all control mechanisms are functioning correctly:

  • All control levers, switches, and pedals operate smoothly and return to neutral when released
  • Emergency stop buttons are accessible and functional
  • Swing brake and swing lock engage and release properly
  • Travel controls respond correctly in all directions
  • Boom hoist and load hoist controls operate without delay or binding

2. Wire Rope and Rigging

Wire rope failures account for a significant percentage of crane incidents. Each shift, inspect the following:

  • Wire rope for visible broken wires, kinks, crushing, birdcaging, or corrosion
  • Proper spooling on drums — no overlapping or uneven winding
  • Rope end connections and terminations are secure
  • Sheaves and pulleys rotate freely without excessive wear or cracks
  • Running ropes for signs of heat damage or abrasion

3. Hydraulic Systems

  • Hydraulic hoses for leaks, bulges, cuts, or abrasion damage
  • Hydraulic fluid levels within manufacturer-specified range
  • Cylinder rods for scoring, pitting, or seal leaks
  • Hydraulic fittings for tightness — no weeping or drips
  • Outrigger cylinders extend and retract fully without drift

4. Safety Devices and Operational Aids

OSHA requires that safety devices installed on the crane be operational before use:

  • Load moment indicator (LMI) or rated capacity indicator (RCI) — powered on and calibrated
  • Anti-two-block device is functional and warning system activates correctly
  • Boom angle indicator reads accurately
  • Horn, alarms, and warning lights are working
  • Wind speed indicator, if equipped, is operational
  • Cameras and monitoring systems, if installed, display correctly

5. Structural Components

  • Boom sections for cracks, dents, bends, or weld failures
  • Turntable and slew ring for unusual play, noise, or visible damage
  • Outrigger beams and floats for cracks, bends, or missing pins
  • Counterweight mounting — secure and all bolts in place
  • Hook for cracks, deformation, throat opening beyond 15% of original, or missing safety latch
  • Hook block for free swivel rotation

6. Electrical Systems

  • All indicator lights and gauges functioning
  • Wiring for fraying, exposed conductors, or loose connections
  • Batteries charged and terminals clean
  • Grounding systems intact
  • Pendant control stations, if used, for button function and cable integrity

7. Tires, Tracks, and Undercarriage

  • Tires for proper inflation, cuts, and excessive wear (mobile cranes)
  • Track tension, pad condition, and roller wear (crawler cranes)
  • Axle and suspension components for damage
  • No fluid leaks under the crane

8. Engine and Power Source

  • Engine oil, coolant, and fuel at proper levels
  • Air filter indicators within acceptable range
  • Belts and hoses for cracking, wear, or looseness
  • Exhaust system for leaks or damage
  • Engine starts and runs without abnormal noise or vibration

9. Operator Cab and Access

  • Windows clean and unobstructed for visibility
  • Mirrors properly positioned
  • Seat belt or restraint system in working condition
  • Steps, ladders, handrails, and walkways secure and free of grease or ice
  • Fire extinguisher present, charged, and within inspection date

Requirements by Crane Type

While the core daily crane inspection checklist applies broadly, different crane types have additional or modified inspection requirements.

Mobile Cranes (Hydraulic Truck Cranes, Rough Terrain, All-Terrain)

Mobile cranes operating under 29 CFR 1926 Subpart CC require particular attention to:

  • Outrigger pad condition and ground bearing capacity at the setup location
  • Tire condition and inflation for travel between lifts
  • Telescoping boom section pins and locks
  • Load charts posted in the cab and matched to the current configuration
  • Carrier and upper structure rotation alignment

Tower Cranes

Tower cranes have unique inspection concerns driven by their height, exposure to weather, and semi-permanent installation:

  • Climbing and anchoring systems for any signs of movement or loosening
  • Trolley travel along the jib — smooth operation, no obstructions
  • Rail clamps and storm locks (for rail-mounted towers)
  • Base and mast connections for bolt tightness and alignment
  • Aviation warning lights operational

Overhead and Gantry Cranes

Overhead cranes in general industry fall under 29 CFR 1910.179 rather than Subpart CC, but daily inspection principles are similar:

  • Bridge and trolley travel limits function correctly
  • Runway rails for obstructions, misalignment, or excessive wear
  • Festoon cables or conductor bars for damage
  • Hoist brake holds load at 125% rated capacity
  • End stops and bumpers in place and undamaged

Who Is Qualified to Perform Daily Crane Inspections?

Under 29 CFR 1926.1412(d), each-shift inspections must be performed by a competent person. OSHA defines a competent person as someone who:

  • Is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions
  • Is authorized to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate those hazards

In practice, this is usually the crane operator. However, the employer is responsible for ensuring that the operator has the knowledge and training to identify the specific hazards associated with the crane type they are inspecting. A competent person for a hydraulic truck crane is not automatically competent for a tower crane.

Some companies designate a dedicated inspector or maintenance technician for daily inspections, especially on larger sites with multiple cranes. Regardless of who performs the inspection, the results must be documented.

Documentation Requirements

OSHA does not prescribe a specific form or format for daily inspection records. However, the regulation does require that:

  • Each-shift inspection results be documented
  • Any deficiencies be reported and corrected before the crane is used
  • Records of inspections be maintained and available for review

At a minimum, your daily crane inspection checklist documentation should include:

  • Date and time of inspection
  • Crane identification (make, model, serial number, or site ID)
  • Name and signature of the inspector
  • Each inspection item checked with a pass/fail notation
  • Description of any deficiencies found
  • Corrective actions taken, including who authorized return to service

During an OSHA crane audit, inspectors will ask to see these records. If you cannot produce them, you have a compliance gap — regardless of whether the inspections were actually performed.

Common Mistakes Operators Make

After reviewing hundreds of inspection programs, these are the errors that show up repeatedly:

Checking Boxes Without Actually Inspecting

The most common problem. An operator fills out the checklist at the start of the shift by marking every item as "pass" without leaving the cab. This defeats the purpose entirely and creates legal liability — a signed checklist with no actual inspection is worse than no checklist at all, because it creates a false record.

Skipping Items Based on Familiarity

Operators who use the same crane every day develop blind spots. They assume that because the wire rope was fine yesterday, it is fine today. Fatigue failures, environmental damage, and gradual wear do not announce themselves — they accumulate between shifts.

Not Reporting Deficiencies

Some operators notice issues but do not report them because they fear the crane will be taken out of service and delay the project. This is a cultural problem that must be addressed at the management level. Operators need to know that reporting a deficiency is always the right call and will never result in blame.

Using Generic Checklists

A daily crane inspection checklist for a 300-ton crawler crane is not the same as one for a 15-ton overhead bridge crane. Using a one-size-fits-all form means critical crane-specific items get missed. Your checklists should be tailored to each crane type and ideally to each specific unit.

Inconsistent Record Keeping

Paper checklists get lost, damaged by weather, or filed inconsistently. When an auditor asks for six months of daily inspection records for a specific crane, many companies cannot produce a complete set. For a detailed comparison of record-keeping methods, see crane inspection software vs paper logs.

How Digital Checklists Improve Compliance

Paper-based daily crane inspection checklists have been the industry standard for decades, but they introduce compliance risk at every step. Digital inspection tools solve the fundamental problems:

Enforced Completeness

A digital checklist can require every item to be addressed before the form can be submitted. Operators cannot skip fields or leave items blank. If a section requires a photo, the system will not accept the inspection without one.

Automatic Timestamps and Location Data

Digital records automatically capture when and where an inspection was completed. This eliminates questions about whether the inspection was actually performed on-site and at the time claimed.

Instant Deficiency Routing

When an operator flags a deficiency on a digital checklist, the system can immediately notify the maintenance team, site supervisor, and safety manager. No one has to physically hand off a paper form or make a phone call. The crane gets locked out of service in the system until the issue is resolved and documented.

Audit-Ready Records

Every inspection is stored, searchable, and exportable. When OSHA requests records for a specific crane over a specific time period, you can produce them in minutes rather than digging through filing cabinets. This is not a minor convenience — it is the difference between a clean audit and a citation.

Crane-Specific Templates

Digital platforms allow you to build inspection templates tailored to each crane type and configuration. A tower crane checklist includes climbing system checks. A mobile crane checklist includes outrigger and tire checks. The operator sees only the items relevant to their specific equipment.

Putting It All Together

A daily crane inspection checklist is not bureaucracy — it is the front line of crane safety. OSHA requires it under 29 CFR 1926.1412 because each-shift inspections catch the problems that develop between periodic and annual inspections. Wire rope degradation. Hydraulic leaks. Control malfunctions. Safety device failures. These are the conditions that lead to dropped loads, structural collapses, and fatalities.

The checklist itself is straightforward. The challenge is execution: making sure every operator inspects every item on every shift and documents it in a way that holds up under scrutiny. That is where process and tooling make the difference.

Replace Paper Checklists with CraneCheck

CraneCheck gives your operators digital daily inspection checklists built to OSHA standards — with enforced completeness, automatic timestamps, instant deficiency alerts, and audit-ready records. Set up your first crane in minutes.
